rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative α-functionalization of ketones via anodic oxidation of silyl enol ethers, enabling C-C bond formation with 1,3-dicarbonyls, α-chlorination, and oxidative [3+2] cycloaddition with styrenes
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the silyl enol ether to a radical cation; a second anodic oxidation of the α-radical intermediate gives an oxocarbenium/oxonium that loses TMS to deliver the α-functionalized ketone. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: α-carbon radical adduct after nucleophilic attack of deprotonated malonate (C)

electron_flow: The anode performs single-electron oxidation of the silyl enol ether (Eox ≈ 1.8 V, lower than malonate at 2.06 V) to give radical cation B; deprotonated malonate attacks the α-carbon to form radical intermediate C, which is oxidized again at the anode to oxonium D; loss of TMS yields the α-functionalized ketone while the cathode evolves H2.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of enol ether 14 vs dimethyl malonate
probe_reagents.observation: Enol ether oxidizes at 1.8 V while Me-malonate oxidizes at 2.06 V, supporting initial anodic oxidation of the enol ether

probe_reagents.name: stoichiometric (1 equiv) Me-malonate experiment
probe_reagents.observation: Formation of ketone dimer side product 18 (confirmed by GCMS), consistent with radical-cation intermediate B reacting with another enol ether

probe_reagents.name: cyclopropyl ring-opening clock (literature precedent, Moeller/Wright)
probe_reagents.observation: Cited prior work establishing enol ether radical cation formation by anodic oxidation

notes: Free ketone is not observed in the reaction mixture, arguing against a pathway via ketone enolization; instead the silyl enol ether is the entry point. The mechanism is shown to be generalizable to α-chlorination (with LiCl as Cl source) and oxidative [3+2] cycloaddition with styrene, both presumed to proceed through the same radical-cation intermediate B.
